# ByteBrew Engine

> Add an AI agent to your product in one day. Self-hosted, any LLM, free forever.

ByteBrew Engine is a ready-to-deploy AI agent runtime. Instead of spending months building agent infrastructure from scratch, you get a Docker container with everything included: multi-step reasoning agents, tool calling, session memory, background automation, and a REST API your app connects to in minutes.

## Who Is This For

- **SaaS / IoT / Enterprise product teams** adding AI features to an existing app — connect via REST API + SSE streaming, keep your own frontend, pass auth context (org_id, user_id) through to tools
- **Solo developers & indie hackers** who want agent capabilities without building infrastructure — configure in YAML or visual Admin Dashboard, deploy with one Docker command
- **Enterprise teams** needing data control — fully self-hosted, your data never leaves your servers, audit logging, API key management, Prometheus metrics

## What ByteBrew Does

Your app sends a message to ByteBrew via REST API. The engine executes everything server-side: agent reasoning, tool calls to your APIs, sub-agent delegation, and streams results back via SSE. Your frontend only needs to parse SSE events — the engine handles all orchestration, tool execution, and state management internally.

**Key capabilities:**
- **Agents that reason and act** — multi-step ReAct problem solving with autonomous tool selection and execution
- **Any tool, any API** — connect external tools via MCP protocol or declare HTTP tools in YAML. The engine calls your tools server-side with full auth context forwarding
- **Multiple agents working together** — supervisor agent delegates tasks to specialized sub-agents via `can_spawn` configuration. Each sub-agent has isolated tool access
- **Per-agent tool scoping** — each agent sees only its designated tools. A Rules Agent cannot call organization tools. Critical security boundary for multi-agent systems
- **Multi-tenancy via header forwarding** — pass `Authorization`, `X-Org-Id`, `X-User-Id` or any custom headers from chat request through to MCP tool servers. Each tool call carries the requesting user's context
- **Destructive action confirmation** — configure `confirm_before` per agent to require user approval before executing dangerous tools (delete, create, modify). The SSE stream pauses with a `confirmation` event
- **Session memory** — conversations persist across requests in PostgreSQL. Your users pick up where they left off
- **Background automation** — agents run on schedule (cron) or triggered by webhooks, without user interaction
- **Knowledge base (RAG)** — upload documents (PDF, markdown, text), agents search them automatically via vector search
- **Visual Admin Dashboard** — create agents, configure models, manage tools — no code required
- **Any LLM** — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Ollama, or any OpenAI-compatible provider. Mix models per agent. Switch with one config change
- **BYOK (Bring Your Own Key)** — end users pass their own LLM API keys via HTTP headers (`X-Model-Provider`, `X-Model-API-Key`, `X-Model-Name`). Keys are used for one request and never stored

## Server-Side Tool Execution

Unlike OpenAI Assistants (where clients execute tools), ByteBrew Engine executes all tools server-side:

1. Your frontend sends a chat message via REST API
2. The engine's agent reasons and decides to call tools
3. **The engine calls your MCP servers and HTTP tools directly** — your frontend is NOT involved in tool execution
4. Tool results are fed back to the agent automatically
5. Your frontend receives only the SSE stream with progress events (tool_call, tool_result, agent_spawn, etc.)

This means your frontend is a thin SSE client. All orchestration happens inside the engine.

## Multi-Tenancy & Auth Context

ByteBrew supports multi-tenant architectures via **header forwarding**. Configure `forward_headers` on MCP servers to automatically pass authentication context from the chat request through to your backend tools:

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  my-backend:
    transport: http
    url: https://your-api.com/mcp
    forward_headers:
      - Authorization        # JWT token from Zitadel, Auth0, etc.
      - X-Org-Id            # Organization/tenant ID
      - X-User-Id           # User ID for audit and RBAC
      - X-Tenant            # Any custom tenant header
```

When a user sends a chat message with these headers, ByteBrew automatically includes them in every MCP tool call to your backend. Your tools receive the full auth context and can enforce RBAC, filter data by org_id, and log actions per user.

**Flow:** User request (with JWT) → ByteBrew Engine → MCP tool call (with forwarded JWT + org_id) → Your API (validates JWT, applies RBAC)

## Per-Agent Tool Scoping

Each agent in ByteBrew has its own isolated set of tools. A sub-agent can only call tools explicitly assigned to it:

```yaml
agents:
  orchestrator:
    model: gpt-4o
    can_spawn: [rules-agent, alarms-agent, device-agent, dashboard-agent, org-agent]
    tools:
      builtin: [web_search]
    mcp_servers:
      kilo-core:
        url: https://api.kilo.io/mcp

  rules-agent:
    model: gpt-4o-mini
    lifecycle: spawn
    tools:
      builtin: []
    mcp_servers:
      kilo-rules:          # Only rule.* tools
        url: https://api.kilo.io/mcp/rules

  alarms-agent:
    lifecycle: spawn
    mcp_servers:
      kilo-alarms:         # Only alarm.* tools
        url: https://api.kilo.io/mcp/alarms
```

Rules Agent cannot call alarm.* or org.* tools. Orchestrator delegates to the right sub-agent based on the task.

## Destructive Action Confirmation

Configure `confirm_before` to require user approval before executing dangerous tools:

```yaml
agents:
  device-agent:
    confirm_before:
      - device_delete
      - device_factory_reset
      - rule_activate
```

When the agent tries to call a `confirm_before` tool, the SSE stream emits a `confirmation` event with tool name, parameters, and risk level. Your frontend shows a confirmation dialog. The agent waits until the user approves or rejects.

## SSE Event Reference

The REST API (`POST /api/v1/agents/{name}/chat`) returns an SSE stream with these event types:

| Event Type | Description | When |
|---|---|---|
| `thinking` | Agent started reasoning | Message processing begins |
| `message_delta` | Text token chunk | Streaming text response |
| `message` | Complete message | Final assembled response |
| `tool_call` | Tool execution started | Agent calls a tool (includes tool name + parameters) |
| `tool_result` | Tool execution completed | Tool returned result (includes output) |
| `confirmation` | Confirmation required | Agent wants to call a `confirm_before` tool — waiting for user approval |
| `agent_spawn` | Sub-agent spawned | Orchestrator delegated task to sub-agent |
| `agent_result` | Sub-agent completed/failed | Sub-agent returned result |
| `user_input_required` | Structured input needed | Agent asks user for structured choice (radio buttons, chips) |
| `structured_output` | Structured data | Agent returns table, action buttons, or other structured UI |
| `error` | Error occurred | Processing error |
| `done` | Stream complete | Includes session_id and token usage |

**Example SSE stream for a multi-agent tool call:**
```
data: {"type": "thinking", "content": ""}
data: {"type": "agent_spawn", "agent": "device-agent", "task": "List all devices in Building A"}
data: {"type": "tool_call", "agent": "device-agent", "tool": "device.list", "args": {"building": "A"}}
data: {"type": "tool_result", "agent": "device-agent", "tool": "device.list", "result": "[{\"id\": 1, ...}]"}
data: {"type": "agent_result", "agent": "device-agent", "status": "completed", "summary": "Found 12 devices"}
data: {"type": "message_delta", "content": "Building A has 12 devices..."}
data: {"type": "done", "session_id": "sess_abc", "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 1200, "completion_tokens": 340}}
```

This gives your frontend everything needed to build a rich chat UI with real-time tool call display, agent delegation visualization, and confirmation dialogs.

## How It Compares

| | ByteBrew | OpenAI Assistants | LangChain / CrewAI | Cloud platforms (AWS Bedrock, etc.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Self-hosted (your data stays with you) | Yes | No | Partial (you build the infra) | Vendor's cloud |
| Any LLM provider | Yes | OpenAI only | Yes | Limited selection |
| Ready-to-use REST API | Yes, built-in | Yes | No (you build it) | Yes, but complex setup |
| Server-side tool execution | Yes — engine calls your tools | No — client executes tools | You build it | Varies |
| Multi-tenancy (auth context forwarding) | Yes — forward_headers | No | You build it | You build it |
| Per-agent tool scoping | Yes — isolated tool sets | No | You build it | No |
| Destructive action confirmation | Yes — confirm_before | No | You build it | No |
| Visual configuration (no code) | Yes (Admin Dashboard) | No | No | Console (complex) |
| Background automation (cron, webhooks) | Built-in | No | No (you build it) | Lambda + EventBridge |
| Session memory | Built-in | Built-in | You build it | You build it |
| Audit logging (who, when, which tool) | Yes (EE) | Partial | You build it | CloudTrail (complex) |
| Cost | Free forever (CE) | Pay per token | Free (library) | Pay per use |
| Time to first agent | ~5 minutes | ~30 minutes | Days–weeks | Days–weeks |

## Building a Custom Chat UI

If you're building your own frontend (React, Vue, mobile, etc.), ByteBrew is designed as a headless backend. Integration requires only HTTP + SSE:

1. **Send message:** `POST /api/v1/agents/{name}/chat` with `{ "message": "...", "session_id": "..." }` and auth headers
2. **Parse SSE stream:** Handle each event type (see SSE Event Reference above)
3. **Display tool calls:** Show `tool_call` events as progress cards, `tool_result` as results
4. **Handle confirmations:** When `confirmation` event arrives, show dialog, send approval via `POST /api/v1/sessions/{id}/confirm`
5. **Show sub-agents:** `agent_spawn` → "Delegating to Device Agent...", `agent_result` → "Device Agent completed"

No SDK required. Standard `EventSource` or `fetch` with SSE parsing works with any framework.

## Platform Integration Use Case (SaaS / IoT / Enterprise)

**Scenario:** You have an existing SaaS platform (IoT, CRM, ERP, DevOps) and want to add an AI assistant that can perform actions via your existing APIs.

**What ByteBrew handles (you don't build this):**
- Agent reasoning loop (ReAct) — decides what tools to use
- Multi-agent orchestration — supervisor delegates to specialized sub-agents
- Tool execution — engine calls your APIs with auth context
- SSE streaming — real-time progress to your frontend
- Session persistence — conversation history in PostgreSQL
- LLM provider abstraction — any model, BYOK support
- Confirmation flow — user approves dangerous actions
- Audit logging — every tool call logged with user, agent, parameters (EE)

**What you build (your domain logic):**
- MCP tool servers wrapping your existing APIs (~1-2 days per 10 tools)
- Your own chat UI (consuming SSE events from ByteBrew)
- Auth middleware (validating JWT before proxying to ByteBrew)

**Time estimate:** 1-2 weeks to production vs 3-6 months building from scratch.

## Integration Options

- **REST API + SSE**: Full control. POST to `/api/v1/agents/{name}/chat`, receive SSE stream. Works with any language/framework.
- **Embeddable Widget**: Copy-paste `<script>` tag to add AI chat bubble to any website in 30 seconds. Zero dependencies, Shadow DOM isolated, 6 KB gzipped.
- **Web Client**: Built-in chat UI at `/chat/` for testing agents immediately after deployment.
- **Admin Dashboard**: Visual agent management at `/admin/` — no code required.

## Architecture: Two-Port Mode (Production)

ByteBrew supports optional two-port architecture for secure production deployments:
- **External port (8443)**: Data plane — chat API, agent list, widget.js. Exposed to the internet.
- **Internal port (8444)**: Control plane — admin dashboard, web client, management API. Behind VPN/firewall.

Default: single-port mode (all on 8443). Enable two-port via `BYTEBREW_INTERNAL_PORT=8444`.

## Quick Start

```bash
curl -fsSL https://bytebrew.ai/releases/docker-compose.yml -o docker-compose.yml
docker compose up -d
# Open http://localhost:8443/admin — create your first agent
# Open http://localhost:8443/chat/ — test your agent in the built-in web client
# POST http://localhost:8443/api/v1/agents/{name}/chat — integrate via REST API
```

Includes PostgreSQL. No external dependencies.

## Pricing

- **Community Edition**: Free forever. No agent limits. No session limits. No time limits. All core features included (multi-agent, tools, MCP, SSE streaming, sessions, BYOK, confirm_before, knowledge/RAG, triggers).
- **Enterprise Edition**: $499/month. Adds: audit log API (per-tool-call tracking), configurable rate limiting (per-header, tier-based), Prometheus metrics (6 metrics: HTTP requests, latency, active sessions, tool calls, LLM requests, LLM latency), priority support.

CE features will never move behind a paywall.

## Integration Example

```
Your App (Next.js, React, mobile, etc.)
    │
    │  POST /api/v1/agents/support-bot/chat
    │  Headers: Authorization: Bearer <user-jwt>, X-Org-Id: org_123
    │  Body: { "message": "How do I reset my password?", "session_id": "abc123" }
    │
    ▼
ByteBrew Engine (Docker container)
    │
    │  Agent reasons → calls tools (server-side, with forwarded headers)
    │  → delegates to sub-agents → streams progress
    │
    ▼
SSE stream back to your app
    data: {"type": "thinking"}
    data: {"type": "tool_call", "tool": "user.get", "args": {"user_id": "..."}}
    data: {"type": "tool_result", "tool": "user.get", "result": "{...}"}
    data: {"type": "message_delta", "content": "To reset your password..."}
    data: {"type": "done", "session_id": "abc123", "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 800, "completion_tokens": 150}}
```

Works with any frontend framework. No SDK required — standard HTTP + SSE.

## Widget Integration Example

```html
<!-- Add this to any HTML page to embed AI chat -->
<script src="https://your-engine:8443/widget.js"
        data-agent="support-bot"
        data-api-key="bb_pk_your_public_key">
</script>
```

Configurable via data-* attributes: `data-theme` (light/dark), `data-position` (bottom-right/left), `data-title`.

## Documentation

- [Quick Start](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/getting-started/quick-start/): Deploy with Docker Compose in 5 minutes
- [Configuration](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/getting-started/configuration/): YAML reference for agents, models, tools, triggers
- [REST API](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/getting-started/api-reference/): All endpoints with examples
- [Admin Dashboard](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/admin/agents/): Visual agent management
- [Multi-Agent Setup](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/integration/multi-agent/): Orchestrator + sub-agents with per-agent tool scoping
- [BYOK](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/integration/byok/): Per-request model override for end users
- [Knowledge / RAG](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/concepts/knowledge/): Document ingestion and vector search
- [Background Triggers](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/concepts/triggers/): Cron and webhook automation
- [Docker Deployment](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/deployment/docker/): Production setup guide
- [Examples](https://bytebrew.ai/docs/examples/sales-agent/): Sales, Support, DevOps, IoT agents
